data.label: Stream clip as fragmented MP4 (PyAV)
data.description: Borrows the uploaded clip from Start via `start.video` (a FileRef), brokers the bytes from the object store to a local path through the sidecar (`aithericon.file(...).retrieve()`), decodes the frames with PyAV and re-muxes them into a FRAGMENTED MP4 / H.264 byte stream (`ftyp` + `moov`(mvex) init segment, then `moof` + `mdat` media fragments), pacing the fragments out over the Data/Out channel `video` in ~real time. Same shape as demo 46 — only the frames are real instead of synthesized. The element content_type is `video/mp4;codecs="avc1.42E01E"`, so the UI render registry (planLiveRender) returns mediaKind=video and the panel can append the stream into a `<video>` element via Media Source Extensions WHILE the detector downstream pulls the same bytes off the data plane. The bulk video rides the out-of-band JetStream datastream transport — it NEVER enters the net marking; the net sees only the channel's open + close.

data.label: Detect objects (YOLO26 + ByteTrack)
data.description: The AI half — the first node in the streaming arc that CONSUMES a data stream and TRANSFORMS it into a control stream. `aithericon.stream('video')` drains the stream step's out-of-band fragmented-MP4 byte stream (starting EARLY, while the producer is still emitting), buffers it into a temp `.mp4`, and runs YOLO26 — Ultralytics' latest SOTA real-time detector (NMS-free / native end-to-end, faster on CPU/edge than YOLO11) — with ByteTrack for stable per-object IDs. Inference runs on the Apple-Silicon GPU via the PyTorch MPS backend (falling back to CPU), comfortably real-time for this clip. Instead of returning one blob, it re-emits each detected object the moment its frame is processed, as a control-plane item on the Control/Out channel `detections` (`aithericon.out('detections')` → open → item* → close): one tiny `{frame,t,label,confidence,bbox,track_id}` token per object. The producer does NOT fold; the consumer edge (`join: gather`) does, in the `summary` step. LIVE-ONLY: the first run pip-installs `ultralytics` (PyTorch + OpenCV, a few hundred MB) and downloads `yolo26n.pt` (~5 MB), which exceeds the 60 s `mekhan test` cap on a cold run; warm runs are fast.

data.label: Summarize detections
data.description: Consumes the detector step's Control channel `detections` with `join: gather`, so the compiler's gather barrier re-orders the emitted detection tokens by index and parks the whole collection as `{ output: [<detection>, ...] }` — fed in as this step's input token. Reads the list off `input.output` and tallies the total object count, a per-class histogram (person/car/truck/bus/…), the number of distinct tracked objects (ByteTrack ids), and the peak per-frame object count. This is the consumer-side fold: the producer just emitted a stream, the join discipline lives on the edge.
